metadata.title: Building & Scaling Open Source Communities with Steve Francia

segments.text: I'm super excited today to have Steve Francia on the podcast.
You know, we'd like to give behind the scenes of sort of the world of development,
use it with the team and VSCO team, but also a major, awesome individual and developer
community kind of get behind the scenes.
I understand how people build software, like how they're evolving as well.
But Steve, one thing I like to do in the podcast that I just started today is read
people's bios from their website because I personally have done this for conferences
and it's always not correct.
So we're going to see how accurate it is today.
Okay, depends which website you're reading it from.
And that's a good question because our bios are all over.
Okay, Steve builds tools that makes developers lives better.
Accurate?
Yeah.
Cool.
He creates open source projects including Hugo and Cobra that have earned over 185,000
GitHub stars.
Maybe a little bit more now.
We shape the modern developer stack from architecting MongoDB's user experience and scaling
go at Google to pioneer container standards at Docker and driving AI at two Sigma.
Is that correct?
That's accurate.
Yeah.
Okay, good.
Yeah.
That's cool.
I mostly have realized that I never update any of my websites.
Yeah.
So you've had a long history of huge projects that you worked on across many different
areas.
Go as a language.
No, I'm not going to be a stack.
I think of these Hugo.
Like how did you actually get involved in these projects?
What led you to working on these projects and building these open source projects?
Yeah, interesting.
It's been a wild ride to be really honest.
There's not a consistent theme, but I started my career mostly in web, consumer facing
things and had the good fortune of being able to be really early adopter of MongoDB,
like really early, like first three adopters of MongoDB.
And that led to getting to know the team and the founders and which led to an experience
of joining very early and having a very formative role in that company and building out
the product and making sure that it had a really stellar user experience as well as a
vibrant community around it.
So we pioneered also a lot of the dev rel things that have become part of the playbook
when we're at MongoDB.
And there I was leading a team about 150 people and some of the other VPs.
I noticed weren't really coding anymore and hadn't coded in a long time.
And I didn't like that for me.
I really wanted to keep my fingers on the pulse of what was changing.
So I was always doing open source stuff to just keep my skill sharp and this led to many
of the other projects that you mentioned.
So I was managing ultimately 12 different languages at MongoDB.
And the last one we added was go of the 12 and I was like, I need a project to learn
this language and I was frustrated with some of the experiences that I was having with
WordPress security and how expensive it was not that it's expensive.
But for me who had to start a content, I was paying a lot to host this dynamic website.
And so my first scope project was Hugo and now it's grown to become the most popular
wish that I generated.
And out of that I knew the experience I wanted.
So I built Cobra to create that experience.
Turns out GO is was really good for the use case of Hugo but it's really good for both
the use case of creating web content as well as CLIs.
And now GO, it's one of the staples of GO companies all over the world use it.
Cooper Netties, GitHub, are all built with Cobra.
How are you balancing life work and open source?
Because I took a lot of open source creators and I am myself as well.
And as these projects grow, it starts off with an idea.
I want to be the thing and then you just put it out there.
And sometimes nothing happens.
You don't use it for yourself, which is some of my favorite software.
It doesn't go to software for myself.
But then all of a sudden, hockey puck, right?
Like what is that balance?
How do you balance that out?
Because not only do you have one big project, you have several of the China Arts projects.
Yeah.
I think a big thing to do is, and I kind of had this epiphany early on was get out of
the way of your project.
What do you mean by that?
Because you want to code it, right?
You want to be honored or don't you?
I do but I realize that some of the projects I have, I'm the only maintainer on and nobody
else uses.
Other projects really become the foundation for a lot of the industry software.
And once that happens, it's not yours anymore.
Got it.
You just got your name on it.
But once Microsoft and Google and GitHub and Amazon, everyone else starts adopting your
project, you can't break the world by making changes to it just because you have
a whim.
So you really have a social contract that we sign as part of participating in open source
that you're going to be responsible for these things.
And I realize that that's a big burden, but there's lots and lots of interested parties
that want to see this succeed.
And so as soon as I saw people were interested sending pull requests, you want to be a
maintainer, you want to help review other people's and really got out of the way.
to let other people do it, give some guidelines, set some goals.
But it's amazing to see what the community does with open source.
So right now I'm not the lead maintainer on any of these projects.
I'm still maintainer on all the projects, but I've asked the torch over to wonderful
maintainers who really have found a lot of pride of maintaining these popular pieces of software
and they're doing a great job and have for years.
You kind of consider yourself sort of like a mentor in that space.
At this point kind of like that or was it sort of a natural like, hey, this is just like
me passing the baton?
I think it's a bit of bulk.
There's things that they're better.
They now know the code base is far better than I do.
They're much closer to working with the latest changes.
They're reviewing all the pull requests that come.
They're really up to speed and fantastic on the coding side.
There's a lot more that go into an open source project than just the code.
And a lot of the other aspects of building the community leadership, marketing, all of
that is an area where I still feel like I can add value and continue to do so.
Some of those things mentoring, but a lot of the things they honestly mentor me.
And so, lean on them for their expertise.
That's probably here about just this evolution of these projects because I think when
I talk to myself included giving up that control, giving up that, hey, I built this thing,
I built it for myself and I wanted this way and now it's not really mine anymore.
I'm actually saying, it's not about me.
It's about the community around it and evolving it.
How did you make that mind shift and maybe the first project for you to say, okay,
this was my thing.
Now, it's kind of my thing because when I talk to a lot of creators, that's the struggle
is I think two-factor.
I don't have the time and the energy and effort.
But I also don't want to give up control of the thing that I wanted because I built this
thing for myself.
Yeah, I think control, this sounds like a pass out of a control is an illusion.
We tell ourselves that we have control of things but as soon as people start adopting
it, you don't have control anymore.
Maybe you do have control but only in ways that can hurt it.
You could do something that would make your project less successful.
But it's like music.
Once a song's written, it doesn't matter what the author's intent was because it matters
the experience of the listener and how they connect to it.
So to think that you own someone else's experiences is weird.
I feel responsibility for these things but I don't feel control.
I never have.
I've always thought, let's collectively do this together and give it the best potential.
Once Kubernetes was a big early user of Cobra and once that happened, it was clear that
the use cases that they had and what they were building with it was far beyond what
I built it for Hugo.
You could also see that this would benefit lots of other companies and projects as it went
on.
It was clear that let's collectively take this in a new direction to push the boundaries
further.
It wasn't about my vision anymore.
It was about my vision would have limited its potential.
Why would I want to limit the potential of this thing that I was fortunate enough to create
and resonated with people?
I think my realization was, why am I getting the way of my own project?
Let me step out of the way, take the ego out and let this project succeed collectively
now as the community itself is building.
Let's talk about that community because I think that's one of the things I love about being
in the developer community space is like amazing developers and it's rich ecosystem.
How did you help or what did you do to have this thriving ecosystem like and especially
for maintainers of open source projects?
I'm ready to start this and I have a few people.
What are the best projects?
What was the first step in helping to build this community around these huge general
and open source projects that it made them continue to be successful?
It's been different for different projects.
There's not a playbook that you can just use.
Our approach for go was very different than MongoDB, very different than Docker and different
from Hugo and Cobra and Viper and all of these are taking different approaches.
Partly because they've peeled the different communities.
The web community is very different than the database community.
Language community is very different.
And so I think there's some common threads to that.
One is at many of the places I've done some similar things.
So at Docker we created the idea of the first ever, I've never heard of anyone else
doing it.
It was a contributorathon.
So we had, I think it was 12 events and 12 cities at the same weekend for the same couple
days.
And we flew out to these places.
We divided up the team.
We flew out to all these places.
We hosted maybe 100 to 300 people in each city.
And we tried to have everybody make their first commit to Docker.
And so we really wanted to show that we're open to contributions.
Later I joined the Go team leading the Go project at Google.
And at Go for Com we decided to do a very similar thing.
We didn't do it in many cities.
We had one of those big rooms at the convention center.
We built a dashboard that put on the wall with a projector.
And everyone's in the same room.
We've got Go members sitting at every table and mentors from the community.
People that were Go members at Google, Go team members at Google and people that were
contributors from the community, mentoring people.
And anyone who went to Go for Com could come and participate.
And the goal is to make your first contribution to the Go project.
We got hundreds of contributions that night to a project that many people
didn't feel like they were either welcomed to contribute or maybe that they,
well, what I can contribute.
I'm not the level of Ken Thompson and Rob Pike to be able to,
I can't contribute to this.
There's a ton of things that entry, people that are young and
their careers, young and their development can do.
There's some things that you need to be far more experienced, but
everyone can contribute.
And that ethos of just like you're welcome here, we want you to be a part of,
there's also two communities.
There's a contributor community and then there's the user community.
Yeah. And those are different approaches.
But for both of them, that spirit of you're welcome here,
we want you to be a part of this and making sure that we have good code of conduct.
And we adhere to that and we have that culture has been a critical part of
growing all these communities.
That's lovely.
Yeah, I think when I talk to a lot of developers,
like that being feeling like they're welcome, right?
I feel like you're allowed to do this.
And I often talk to community members and developers and say,
listen, it doesn't matter if it's adding to documentation,
fixing the typo, fixing up the read, me, making,
you know, doing an issue, right?
Just even posting that, that's a contribution to itself.
Like I don't great bug report or following up or doing something.
And then even just saying, hey, I'll take this on.
It could be small.
It could be little every single contributions.
Welcome.
Is there things that you did maybe in the repo level that like,
you said, you know, the standards and things like, yeah, maybe not just in
the repo level, like from the issues or the poor requests,
like that maybe folks could that would be worked or then didn't work.
Yeah.
That maybe folks could maybe adopt that would like encourage that type of
contributions.
So it depends on the scale of the project.
So for the GOAP project, we did some very specific things to encourage this.
For some of the smaller relatively smaller projects like Cobra,
we haven't done these.
And I think the level of contribution there is a little more targeted.
It's just, you know, the scope, not the user base is different,
but just the scope GOA is an editor, compiler.
I mean, it's got the full scope of everything about the powers,
and the entire GOECA system is within that one project.
So for this, we have a special tag,
a label that we put on issues that are beginner issues.
Part of our dashboard that we made and is still there is that you can go and
find beginner issues.
And so we really wanted to say like new contributions.
Welcome.
Really tried to make it good for them.
We also built, we wrote guides and a tool that helps people make their first
contributions because we had a bit of a specific workflow of how we do things.
And so I think brilliant idea was to create, was not my idea.
So my idea was to polish up our guides and we did and they're really good.
But an even better idea was to write a tool that guided it, you through it,
step by step.
So it's interactive CLI tool that just, okay, step one and then it took you on
that step and it had you log in and do things and then step two.
So it was very sequential and rather than reading something and then coming
over here and doing something, you stayed in your terminal and it stepped you
through all the things that you needed to do interactively.
As you had more people contribute to these projects, one thing that I often
hear from maintainers, but also myself, I'm saying maintainers, but I'm actually
just most referring to myself as a maintainer, but you know, it worked at huge
projects here at Microsoft is as you get more contributions, you get more
users, you get more issues, you get more pull requests.
How did you balance that to grow this community out as more things were coming
in because the overhead of saying, I just actually like an issue
with triage and a pull request reviewer at the end of the day compared to
like I get to write code, right?
Or wow, someone did a PR, but we're so swamped that actually is going to take
a week or two weeks and that can demotivate people as well.
How do you balance that in these projects as they continue to grow?
First, I think it's one of the biggest challenges.
I think people dramatically underestimate the maintenance cost
of creating software.
And maintainer burdens big.
We had Docker, we had a dramatically oversized pool of contributions.
We had developed very specific strategies that were somewhat unique to
Docker and the challenges that we had there.
Go similar thing, we actually had LGTM bot that came and did things.
So we built some different really agents that would connect to things,
look for different triggers and analyze the content and just help facilitate
the human aspect of it.
The project that I've been involved with that I think did this the best and still is the
gold standard for a contributor, a contributor of community is Drupal.
Most people I don't think are aware that Drupal is the second most active
open source project in history.
Oh wow, I didn't know.
So Linux kernel has the most active contributors and Drupal is number two.
And they just do a fabulous job of different roles.
There's a lot of interest in contributing and being part of Drupal,
but they have different roles that you can take.
So one of the roles is just a triager.
And that's how you kind of get your feet wet with the project and start really understanding it
better.
And so as each as you kind of grow in contributions, you get more things that you can do,
but they really distribute the burden and load of this across a broad group of people.
And what they've created is this pipeline that as new people are always open source and everything
in life, like comes out to different events happen.
You might have a kid or an illness or whatever it is or a new job that has taken you out of the
pipeline.
But new people are coming into the pipeline.
And so you want to make sure that they get the right training and continue that to go through it.
And so I've learned a lot from Drupal.
I had a benefit of being a part of the Drupal Association board for many years.
There's a way to contribute back from all the things I learned from how well they did with this.
And I hands off to Dries and Angie and the rest of the folks that really built this amazing
contributor community there.
Yeah.
I love that journey because just like how we were talking about high,
any contribution matters.
Also, any of those contributions, any of those levels, actually just reviewing the PRs and going through
and reviewing the issues.
I think what's fascinating, a lot of friends that I work with is many of them are like,
I love developing, but what I really like doing is writing amazing documentation.
That's what I love doing.
Like let me just do that.
And that's it.
And you focus on the code, all right, the docs writer.
People are like, I'm really good at marketing, right?
And like for developer marketing, even though they're an engineer, this is actually what I want
to do in the social project.
And all of those are super valuable contributions.
Yeah.
I think one thing that I'm fascinating because it wouldn't be the year 2025,
I would talk about a little bit of AI.
But actually like in these open source projects, as we have more agents,
we have more automated pull requests.
Like how is that evolving?
How you think about building the communities and continuing to grow these projects
as just the entire sort of software engineering cycle is going through like this transformation,
especially for open source projects.
Yeah.
It's a really interesting question.
And I think my personal opinion about this is, and it's confusing.
Like I don't think we have a good sense of how it's going to evolve.
There's definitely a sense of the more things that we give to robots, the less involved humans are,
which is a challenging thing because I think the human experience is to create.
I think that's a fundamental part of the human experience.
And now we've got robots creating things.
And it's really changing it.
It's changing the shape of the community.
I think a key part of it is to find the right balance between doing creative things
that you love and are passionate about and having robots help facilitate and assist that.
And I don't think we have the right answer to what that looks like yet.
I think the more automated these things are, kind of the less human experience is part of it.
But at the same time, we've been using machines the whole time to automate different aspects of it
and to make it better for us.
So I think we've really got to figure out what the right balance is across those.
And I think it's changing not only the software development life cycle,
it's also changing how we find new packages.
Right? Because nowadays most of the time, the alarms are making the suggestion.
You're not going to GitHub or Google or, you know, like the Go.dev webpage.
You're going to your terminal or to your editor.
And it's suggesting when it builds this function, it's putting the import statement in there for you.
Yeah. So it's really fundamentally changing how we approach things.
It's also like a lot of times now you're not reading the documentation,
but an agent's reading the documentation.
And so how are we adapting our projects to be able to fit into this new structure where
agents might be the primary audience.
I think it's going to really, we'll see how it plays out over the next few months,
but it's certainly something that we need to think about.
Yeah. And sometimes they're writing the documentation.
A lot of times they're writing.
Yeah. Well, let me wrap up with this because this has been really awesome.
I really appreciate it because every day I'm working in huge projects here,
like yourself and outside of work as well and being about how do I balance everything
and how you've done it and these projects and work with the communities.
And there's so many different unique ways.
I think what I'm learning at least is they're not one approach.
There's many approaches like pool or spouse for you in this.
Well, as you just excited about the future of open source,
I mean, it's been a while journey, I have to imagine since the beginning of kind of working
in these projects. Well, how's your excited for the future of open source going forward?
So I think we're at a really interesting time. A really exciting time.
We've got this new technology that the LMs that can do things that we've struggled with
since the beginning of computing. And figuring out how to balance this new incorporate,
this new capability that we didn't have before into things, into how we've done things before.
And it really unlocks a lot of potential. And I'm really excited to see how that evolves
to the developer experience, how it involves the open source experience.
I think there's just a ton of potential that we're barely scratched the surface of how really this
integration looks. And I'm also excited that we can do things that we've never done before.
Yeah. Things that we've even have the time to code before. Now, we have more time to code
because the assistants accelerate what we're doing, as well as it's really giving us opportunities
to write software that before there wasn't this capability of a lot of the old methods of understanding,
these like flexible content, whether images or other things, just wasn't available. And now
we've got, it's been unlocked by these multimodal models that can really understand things in a
way that would have been very challenging before. So I think it's a very exciting time where we're
able to build things faster than we've ever built before and unlock new capabilities. And I'm excited
to see what the world does with it. That's awesome. Yeah. For me for sure, I've definitely written
more software faster than I ever have in my life. And stuff that I wouldn't have built. I think
that's what's interesting too is I may not have had the time to build that feature, build that
little project. And somebody's capability just enable me to do it, right? Whether it's short-lived
or long-lived. Yeah. We'll see things so much for coming on the path. Perfect. Thank you.
I'm not always, I really enjoyed it. We'll put links to everything. Find all your projects and
everything in the show. That'll be great. So thanks again for coming on. Appreciate it. Thank you. I appreciate it.
